dev_plans.content: Lightweight kNN batch-mixing heuristic on the scVI 10-d latent space. WHAT: a fast nearest-neighbour mixing score that approximates iLISI without external integration libraries. WHERE: insert as a callback at the end of every training epoch (and once post-training). After the epoch, run model.get_latent_representation() on a fixed held-out subsample of cells (e.g. 2000 cells, stratified so both PBMC batches are represented). STEPS: (1) Stash the batch label vector b (0/1 for the two PBMC batches) for the subsample once at setup. (2) Each epoch, embed the subsample to Z (n x 10). (3) Build a kNN graph on Z with k=30 using sklearn.neighbors.NearestNeighbors (euclidean). (4) For each cell, compute the fraction of its k neighbours whose batch label differs from its own. (5) Define mixing = mean over cells of (observed_other_batch_fraction / expected_other_batch_fraction), where expected = global fraction of the opposite batch; clip per-cell ratio to [0,1] then average so 1.0 = neighbourhoods match the global batch composition (perfect mixing) and 0.0 = fully segregated. (6) Log the scalar to the run logger and append to a CSV; render a per-epoch time-series PNG with the two threshold lines. OUTPUT: epoch-indexed CSV column `knn_batch_mixing`, a line plot, and a console alert if the final value falls below the acceptable line. This is O(n log n) and adds negligible epoch overhead.
dev_plans.metric: kNN batch-mixing score per epoch (mean over cells of clipped observed/expected opposite-batch neighbour fraction at k=30), float in [0,1], higher is better
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.90 — a well-integrated 2-batch PBMC scVI run should have local neighbourhoods almost matching the global batch composition; 0.9 reflects near-complete mixing reported by competitive scVI/Harmony integrations on PBMC.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.75 — neighbourhoods are mostly mixed with mild residual structure, the soft target a decent run reaches before diminishing returns.

dev_plans.content: Statistically rigorous kBET acceptance rate on the latent space. WHAT: implement the k-nearest-neighbour Batch-Effect Test (Buttner 2019) directly — a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test that, for many random neighbourhoods, asks whether local batch composition matches the global composition. WHERE: end-of-epoch callback plus a final post-training computation, on a fixed stratified subsample (e.g. 2000 cells) for runtime stability. STEPS: (1) At setup record global batch frequencies f = (f0, f1) and batch labels. (2) Each epoch embed the subsample to Z (n x 10). (3) Build a kNN graph with neighbourhood size k0 = max(10, floor(0.10*n)). (4) Sample N test neighbourhoods (e.g. 500 random centre cells). (5) For each neighbourhood compute the chi-squared statistic stat = sum_b ((observed_b - k0*f_b)^2 / (k0*f_b)) over the two batches; compare to chi2 with 1 dof. (6) A neighbourhood 'rejects' if its p-value < 0.05 (local composition deviates from global, i.e. batch effect present). (7) kBET acceptance rate = 1 - (rejection fraction); higher = better mixing. (8) Average over a few random seeds for the subsample to reduce variance. OUTPUT: epoch-indexed `kbet_acceptance` in a CSV, a time-series plot with standard/acceptable dashed lines, and the per-epoch mean rejection rate logged for diagnostics. Uses only numpy/scipy/sklearn so it stays dependency-light while being the formally defined kBET statistic.
dev_plans.metric: kBET acceptance rate per epoch (1 minus fraction of random k-neighbourhoods rejecting the chi-squared homogeneity test at alpha=0.05, k0=10% of subsample), float in [0,1], higher is better
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.75 — kBET is strict and rarely saturates near 1 even for good integrations; 0.75 acceptance is at the upper end of what well-tuned scVI achieves on 2-batch PBMC per scIB benchmarks.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.55 — a moderate acceptance rate indicating substantial but imperfect mixing, the realistic floor for a competent run given kBET's strictness.

dev_plans.content: Library-based scIB integration metrics (iLISI + batch ASW). WHAT: use the maintained scib package (Luecken 2022) to compute the canonical integration metrics on the latent embedding, avoiding hand-rolled statistics. WHERE: end-of-epoch callback (or every N epochs if runtime matters) plus a final post-training run, on a fixed stratified subsample. STEPS: (1) pip install scib (and scanpy/anndata). (2) At setup build an AnnData with the batch label in adata.obs['batch']. (3) Each epoch, write the scVI latent into adata.obsm['X_emb'] via model.get_latent_representation() on the subsample. (4) Compute iLISI with scib.metrics.ilisi_graph(adata, batch_key='batch', type_='embed', use_rep='X_emb'); scib already rescales iLISI to [0,1] where 1 = perfect mixing. (5) Compute batch ASW with scib.metrics.silhouette_batch(adata, batch_key='batch', label_key=<celltype or a single dummy label>, embed='X_emb'); scib returns 1-|ASW| so that 1 = batches indistinguishable (ASW near 0) and lower = residual batch separation. (6) Record both, and report the primary scalar as the scib-scaled iLISI. (7) Log to CSV, plot the iLISI time series with both threshold lines, and also chart batch-ASW as a secondary panel. OUTPUT: epoch-indexed `ilisi_scib` and `batch_asw_scib` columns, two plots, and a PASS/FAIL flag on iLISI vs the standard line. This is the most faithful to the published benchmarking methodology and the easiest to defend against reviewers, at the cost of heavier dependencies and per-epoch graph construction.
dev_plans.metric: scIB-scaled iLISI per epoch (scib.metrics.ilisi_graph on the 10-d embedding, rescaled to [0,1]), higher is better
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.80 — scaled iLISI of 0.8 corresponds to strong batch mixing in scIB's PBMC benchmarks for top integration methods; strict but reachable by well-tuned scVI.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.60 — good-not-great mixing; scaled iLISI around 0.6 is what a solid baseline integration typically lands at before diminishing returns.
